# Reserves and fund balance

A school district's **reserves** and **fund balance** are its savings — money set
aside from prior years that is not yet spent. They are a buffer for cash-flow
gaps, a cushion against unexpected costs, and a way to save for planned future
expenses. The size of a district's reserves is also one of the clearest signs of
its fiscal health: too little leaves it exposed; the state limits how much of the
flexible kind a district may hold.

This chapter explains the different kinds of reserves a New York school district
keeps, the legal cap on the flexible part, how districts across the state sit
relative to that cap, and how Cambridge's own reserves have changed. It draws on
the official chart of accounts (kept in this repo's [`docs/`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/tree/main/docs)
folder), NYSED's Property Tax Report Card, OSC school-district finance data, and
Cambridge's audited annual financial reports.

## Two kinds of "reserves": reserve funds and fund balance

The word "reserves" gets used two ways, and the distinction matters.

**Reserve funds** are accounts created under specific state laws, each for a
specific purpose. The money in them is **restricted** — it can only be used for
that purpose. A New York school district's main reserve funds are:

| Reserve fund | Account code | Legal basis | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capital reserve | A878 | Education Law §3651 | Cost of items for which bonds may be issued (requires voter approval) |
| Tax certiorari reserve | A864 | General Municipal Law §3651.1-a | Property-tax refund judgments (mandatory; capped at anticipated claims) |
| Retirement contribution reserve (ERS / TRS) | A827 / A828 | General Municipal Law §6-r | Pension contributions to the state retirement systems |
| Employee benefit accrued liability reserve | A867 | General Municipal Law §6-p | Accrued employee benefits payable on termination |
| Insurance reserve | A863 | General Municipal Law §6-m | Liability, property, and casualty deductibles/losses |
| Repair reserve | A878-type | General Municipal Law §6-d | Non-recurring repairs to capital improvements |
| Unemployment insurance reserve | A815 | — | Unemployment-insurance claims |
| Workers' compensation reserve | A814 | — | Workers'-compensation claims |
| Reserve for debt | A884 | — | Debt-service requirements |

**Fund balance** is the broader accounting term for the net resources of a fund
after liabilities. The General Fund's fund balance is classified into:

- **Restricted** — the reserve funds above (each legally earmarked).
- **Committed** — set aside by board action for a specific purpose.
- **Assigned** — intended for a particular use (e.g., **appropriated** into the
  next year's budget, or **unappropriated**).
- **Unassigned** — the residual, general-purpose balance with no specific
  designation. This is the flexible "rainy-day" money.

So the relationship is: **reserve funds are the *restricted* portion of fund
balance.** A district can hold large amounts in restricted reserves and still
have little *unassigned* balance — and it is the unassigned balance that the law
caps.

## The 4% cap

New York's property-tax-cap law (Real Property Tax Law §2-c / Education Law
§2023-a) limits a school district's **unassigned (unexpended-surplus) fund
balance** to **4% of the prior year's budget**. This is the measure NYSED reports
in the Property Tax Report Card as `fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget`.

The cap applies **only to the unassigned balance**, not to the restricted reserve
funds. That is why a district with substantial capital, retirement, or tax-
certiorari reserves is not "over the cap" — those are restricted for their
purposes. The 4% rule constrains only the flexible money a district can keep on
hand.^[The cap on the property-tax *levy* itself is a separate rule, covered in
the Taxes chapter and the contingency-budgets chapter.]

## Why districts build specific reserves

Each reserve fund exists to set aside money for a **specific, often lumpy or
unpredictable cost** the district is legally required to pay — smoothing a bill
that would otherwise hit the budget all at once. The clearest example is the
**retirement reserve**. A district's pension cost depends on an annually-set
employer contribution rate that swings widely: NYSTRS's teachers'-system rate has
ranged from roughly **6% to 17.5% of payroll** since 2014-15 (it was **16.25% for
2023-24**, for example).^[NYSTRS employer contribution rate history,
<https://www.nystrs.org/employers/employer-contribution-rate/>. The rate is
smoothed over several years of investment returns, so market shocks feed in
slowly — hence the run-up to 16.25% after the 2022 market decline.] By
contributing to the reserve in lower-rate years, a district builds a cushion it
can draw on when the rate spikes — instead of cutting programs or sharply raising
taxes that year. The same logic runs through the other reserves: each earmarks
savings for a cost that is mandatory, large, and hard to time — tax-refund
judgments, insurance or workers'-comp claims, capital projects.

A second reason matters under the tax cap: **restricted reserve funds are not
subject to the 4% unassigned-fund-balance limit**. Building reserves is therefore
the main way a district holds savings *beyond* that 4% flexible ceiling — the
trade-off being that the money is locked to its specific purpose.

## Using reserves for budget relief

A district spends down a reserve by **appropriating** it — booking a planned draw
as a revenue source in the coming year's budget. When, say, $X is appropriated
from the TRS reserve toward that year's pension bill, the budget needs $X less
from taxes and state aid for the TRS line, **freeing room for other spending, for
tax relief, or to rebuild other reserves**. Cambridge's board did this for
2025-26, appropriating **$625,000 from the capital reserve** and **$200,119 from
the employee-benefit reserve**.

Two points keep this honest:

- **It is not new money.** The reserve was funded from *prior* years' taxes, so
  drawing it shifts cost backward and leaves less cushion for the next shock — it
  smooths timing; it does not create resources.
- **It works within the cap.** Using reserves as a revenue source lowers the levy
  the budget requires — a key lever for staying under the tax cap, though leaning
  on it too hard erodes the buffer.

Reserve funds can be spent **only for their statutory purpose** — a TRS reserve
cannot buy a roof, and a capital reserve cannot pay a pension bill. Several carry
extra rules (from the chart of accounts and Cambridge's audited reports):

- **Capital reserve (Education Law §3651):** both establishing it *and* every
  expenditure from it require voter approval by proposition.
- **Tax certiorari reserve (General Municipal Law §3651.1-a):** mandatory to fund;
  the balance may not exceed anticipated judgments and claims, and any unused
  amount must be returned to the General Fund by the first day of the fourth
  fiscal year after deposit. (Cambridge was flagged in 2024-25 for over-funding
  this reserve; it was corrected.)
- **Retirement reserve (General Municipal Law §6-r):** a board may contribute up
  to **2% of covered salaries** per year, with the TRS sub-fund capped at **10%**
  of covered salaries.
- **Employee benefit reserve (General Municipal Law §6-p):** established by board
  vote; for accrued benefits payable on termination.

The **unassigned** balance, by contrast, has no purpose restriction — it is the
flexible money — which is precisely why the state caps it at 4%.

## Fund balance across districts vs. the cap

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import os
import sys
from pathlib import Path

sys.path.insert(0, os.getcwd())

import polars as pl
from plotnine import (
    aes, geom_hline, geom_line, geom_point, geom_ribbon, ggplot, labs,
    scale_x_continuous, scale_y_continuous, theme,
)
from great_tables import GT, loc, md, style

import comparisons as cc

# Locate the repo's data/ dir (Quarto may run from the book dir or project root).


def _find_repo_root() -> Path:
    cwd = Path.cwd()
    for base in (cwd, *cwd.parents):
        if (base / "data" / "crosswalks").is_dir():
            return base
    raise FileNotFoundError("Could not find repo root")


REPO_ROOT = _find_repo_root()
PTRC_PATH = REPO_ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "property_tax_report_card.parquet"
CAM_FINANCE_PATH = REPO_ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "cambridge_finance.parquet"

#: account code -> reserve type label (OSC chart of accounts, A8xx)
RESERVE_CODES = {
    "A815": "Unemployment insurance",
    "A827": "Retirement — ERS",
    "A828": "Retirement — TRS",
    "A864": "Tax certiorari",
    "A867": "Employee benefit accrued liability",
    "A878": "Capital reserve",
    "A884": "Reserve for debt",
}
```

**Most New York districts hold their unassigned fund balance right at the 4%
ceiling.** The chart below shows, for each year, the middle half of districts
(shaded band) and the median, against the 4% cap and Cambridge.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fund-balance-vs-cap
#| fig-cap: "Unassigned (unexpended-surplus) fund balance as a percent of each district's budget, school years ending 2015–2027. Shaded band = middle 50% of districts; solid line = median; the dashed line is the 4% legal cap. Cambridge is the red line. Source: NYSED Property Tax Report Card."
fb = pl.read_parquet(PTRC_PATH).select(
    "year_end", "nysed_district_cd", "fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget"
).drop_nulls("fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget")

band = (
    fb.group_by("year_end").agg(
        pl.col("fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget").quantile(0.25).alias("p25"),
        pl.col("fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget").median().alias("median"),
        pl.col("fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget").quantile(0.75).alias("p75"),
    ).sort("year_end")
)
cam = (
    pl.read_parquet(PTRC_PATH)
    .filter(pl.col("nysed_district_cd") == "64161004")
    .select("year_end", "fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget")
    .sort("year_end")
)

(
    ggplot()
    + geom_ribbon(band, aes("year_end", ymax="p75", ymin="p25"), fill="#C8D9E6", alpha=0.8)
    + geom_line(band, aes("year_end", "median"), color="#2C6E9B", size=1)
    + geom_hline(yintercept=4, linetype="dashed", color="#555555")
    + geom_line(cam, aes("year_end", "fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget"),
                color="#C8102E", size=1.6)
    + geom_point(cam, aes("year_end", "fund_balance_unrestricted_pct_of_budget"),
                 color="#C8102E", size=1.8)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2015, 2028)))
    + scale_y_continuous(breaks=list(range(0, 11, 2)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Unassigned fund balance (% of budget)",
        title="District unassigned fund balance vs. the 4% cap, 2014–15 to 2026–27",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
    + theme(figure_size=(9, 4.5))
)
```

The **median district reports exactly 4% every year** — the cap binds, and
districts keep the maximum flexible balance the law allows. A meaningful tail
sits above 4% in some years (the top of the band reaches about 6% in the early
2020s), and roughly **a quarter to a third of districts report over 4%** in a
given year. **Cambridge tracks right at the cap** (about 3–4.6%), a typical
position.

## Total reserves vs. other districts

The 4% chart above covers only the *unassigned* balance. A district's **total**
fund balance — the restricted reserve funds plus assigned and unassigned balance —
is a fuller measure of its savings. As a share of the budget, Cambridge sits
**below** the typical New York district.

```{python}
#| label: fig-total-fb-comparative
#| fig-cap: "Total General Fund fund balance (restricted reserve funds + assigned + unassigned) as a percent of each district's budget, school years ending 2015–2027. Shaded band = middle 50% of districts; solid line = median; Cambridge is the red line. Source: NYSED Property Tax Report Card."
tfb = (
    pl.read_parquet(PTRC_PATH)
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("fund_balance_restricted").fill_null(0)
         + pl.col("fund_balance_assigned_appropriated").fill_null(0)
         + pl.col("fund_balance_unrestricted").fill_null(0)).alias("total_fb"),
    )
    .filter(pl.col("proposed_spending") > 0)
    .with_columns((pl.col("total_fb") / pl.col("proposed_spending") * 100).alias("fb_pct"))
)
band2 = (
    tfb.group_by("year_end").agg(
        pl.col("fb_pct").quantile(0.25).alias("p25"),
        pl.col("fb_pct").median().alias("median"),
        pl.col("fb_pct").quantile(0.75).alias("p75"),
    ).sort("year_end")
)
cam2 = (
    tfb.filter(pl.col("nysed_district_cd") == "64161004")
    .select("year_end", "fb_pct").sort("year_end")
)

(
    ggplot()
    + geom_ribbon(band2, aes("year_end", ymax="p75", ymin="p25"), fill="#C8D9E6", alpha=0.8)
    + geom_line(band2, aes("year_end", "median"), color="#2C6E9B", size=1)
    + geom_line(cam2, aes("year_end", "fb_pct"), color="#C8102E", size=1.6)
    + geom_point(cam2, aes("year_end", "fb_pct"), color="#C8102E", size=1.8)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2015, 2028)))
    + scale_y_continuous(breaks=list(range(0, 61, 10)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Total fund balance (% of budget)",
        title="Total fund balance vs. other districts, 2014–15 to 2026–27",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
    + theme(figure_size=(9, 4.5))
)
```

The typical New York district holds total fund balance worth **about a quarter
of its budget** (the median rose from ~19% in 2014–15 to ~26% by the mid-2020s).
Districts broadly built up savings over the period. **Cambridge sits below that
median** — about 11% of budget in 2014–15, rising to ~19% by 2024–25, around the
**27th percentile statewide**. Cambridge's reserves grew *faster* than the typical
district's over the decade, but from a thinner base, so they remain **comparatively
modest**.

## Cambridge's reserves by type, over time

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cambridge-reserves
#| tbl-cap: "Cambridge Central School District reserve fund balances by type, school years ending 2013–2025, in thousands of dollars. Source: NYS Comptroller school-district finance data (ST-3 account codes A8xx). Blank = reserve not held that year."
fin = pl.read_parquet(CAM_FINANCE_PATH)
years = list(range(2013, 2026))


def _amt(code: str, year: int):
    """End-of-year balance ($, or None) for one account code in one year."""
    s = fin.filter((pl.col("ACCOUNT_CODE") == code) & (pl.col("CALENDAR_YEAR") == year)).select("AMOUNT").to_series()
    return s.first() if s.len() else None


rows = []
for code, label in RESERVE_CODES.items():
    rows.append({"Reserve": label, **{str(y): (_amt(code, y) or 0) / 1000 for y in years}})

res_df = pl.DataFrame(rows)
res_df = pl.concat([
    res_df,
    pl.DataFrame([{"Reserve": "Total restricted reserves",
                   **{str(y): sum(filter(None, (_amt(c, y) for c in RESERVE_CODES))) / 1000
                      for y in years}}]),
    pl.DataFrame([{"Reserve": "Unassigned (4%-capped)",
                   **{str(y): (_amt("A917", y) or 0) / 1000 for y in years}}]),
    pl.DataFrame([{"Reserve": "Total General Fund balance",
                   **{str(y): (_amt("A8029", y) or 0) / 1000 for y in years}}]),
], how="vertical")

year_cols = [str(y) for y in years]
bold_rows = pl.col("Reserve").is_in(["Total restricted reserves", "Total General Fund balance"])

(
    GT(res_df, rowname_col="Reserve")
    .tab_header(
        title="Cambridge's reserve funds by type",
        subtitle="School years ending 2013 (2012–13) through 2025 (2024–25), $ thousands",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols, decimals=0)
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.text(weight="bold"), style.fill(color="#FCEFB4")],
        locations=loc.body(rows=bold_rows),
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NYS Comptroller local-government finance data (ST-3), account "
            "codes A814–A884 (reserve funds), A917 (unassigned), A8029 (total General "
            "Fund fund balance, end of year). Year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25). "
            "Figures are as-filed (unaudited); they reconcile with Cambridge's audited "
            "reports (see below)."
        )
    )
)
```

Cambridge's reserves have **grown and diversified** over the decade:

- The **total General Fund balance roughly doubled**, from about **$2.5 million
  in 2012–13 to $5.2 million in 2024–25**.
- The district **added reserves it previously did not hold** — a **TRS
  (teachers') retirement** reserve from 2020–21, an **employee-benefit accrued
  liability** reserve, and a substantial **capital reserve** (~$1.1 million) from
  2021–22.
- The **ERS retirement** reserve roughly doubled (to ~$884,000 in 2024–25); the
  **reserve for debt** was drawn down to zero as debt retired.
- The **unassigned, 4%-capped** balance — the flexible part — has been about
  **$1.0–1.2 million** in recent years, sitting at the cap.

## What Cambridge's audited reports show

Cambridge's audited annual financial reports (filed with the Federal Audit
Clearinghouse) confirm and add color to these figures. For **2024–25** they report
**total restricted fund equity of about $2.95 million**, broken down as:

| Reserve (2024–25) | Amount |
|---|---|
| Capital reserves | $1,105,710 |
| Retirement contribution — ERS | $884,019 |
| Retirement contribution — TRS | $663,060 |
| Employee benefit accrued liability | $147,207 |
| Tax certiorari | $138,351 |
| School lunch (encumbrances) | $8,098 |
| **Total restricted** | **$2,946,445** |

This reconciles with the ST-3 figures in the table above (the audited total is
~$8,000 higher, the school-lunch encumbrance). The reports also note the reserves
the board has **appropriated** (budgeted to spend) for 2025–26: **$625,000 from
the capital reserve** and **$200,119 from the employee-benefit reserve**.

The audits flagged two reserve-related items, both since corrected:

- **Tax-certiorari reserve over-funding (finding 2024-002).** The reserve
  exceeded the amount of anticipated tax-certiorari claims. General Municipal Law
  §3651.1-a caps this reserve at anticipated judgments and refunds, and any
  unused balance must be returned to the General Fund. Cambridge corrected the
  over-funding.
- A **capital-assets / net-debt** presentation issue (finding 2024-001, also
  corrected), where invested-in-capital-assets-net-of-related-debt appeared
  negative.

Taken together, Cambridge's reserves are healthy and growing, concentrated in
restricted funds tied to specific future costs (capital projects, pensions),
while its flexible unassigned balance is held at the legal 4% ceiling.

*Sources: OSC [Accounting & Reporting Manual](https://www.osc.ny.gov/files/local-government/publications/pdf/accounting-and-reporting-manual-for-school-districts.pdf)
(chart of accounts); NYSED Property Tax Report Card; NYS Comptroller school-
district finance data (ST-3); Cambridge CSD audited annual financial reports
(Federal Audit Clearinghouse, 2020–2025). Reference copies are in the repo's
[`docs/`](https://github.com/donboyd5/ccs/tree/main/docs) folder.*
